function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

REPLACE MODE (mode='replace', default): find a unique string and replace it. REQUIRED PARAMETERS: mode, path, old_string, new_string.
PATCH MODE (mode='patch'): apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes. REQUIRED PARAMETERS: mode, patch.

mode.description: Edit mode. 'replace' (default): requires path + old_string + new_string. 'patch': requires patch content only.

path.description: REQUIRED when mode='replace'. File path to edit.

old_string.description: REQUIRED when mode='replace'. Exact text to find and replace. Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include surrounding context lines to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: REQUIRED when mode='replace'. Replacement text. Pass empty string '' to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
REQUIRED when mode='patch'. V4A format patch content. Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits. Auto-runs syntax checks on .py/.json/.yaml/.toml and other linted languages; only NEW errors introduced by this write are surfaced (pre-existing errors are filtered out).

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"path": "/home/admin/.hermes/profiles/pikachu/skills/software-development/code-review/SKILL.md", "content": "---
name: code-review
description: Code review checklist covering security, performance, maintainability, and best practices
author: Pikachu
tags: [code-review, quality]
team: pikachu
---

# Code Review Skill

A comprehensive code review framework covering security, performance, style, and the PR review workflow.

---

## Security Review Checklist

### Authentication & Authorization
- [ ] All endpoints/operations enforce proper authentication
- [ ] Authorization checks use least-privilege principle — users can only access their own resources unless explicitly designed otherwise
- [ ] No hardcoded secrets, API keys, tokens, or passwords in code, configs, or comments
- [ ] Session tokens and JWTs are properly validated (expiration, signature, audience)
- [ ] Role-based access controls (RBAC) are consistently applied

### Input Validation & Sanitization
- [ ] All user-supplied input is validated (type, length, range, format)
- [ ] SQL queries use parameterized statements or an ORM — no raw string concatenation
- [ ] Command injection vectors are eliminated (avoid `os.system()`, `subprocess(shell=True)`, `eval()`, `exec()`)
- [ ] File uploads are validated (type, size, path traversal prevention)
- [ ] All output is properly encoded to prevent XSS (HTML entity encoding, CSP headers)

### Data Protection
- [ ] Sensitive data (PII, credentials, financial) is encrypted at rest and in transit
- [ ] Logging does not expose secrets, tokens, or personal data
- [ ] Error messages shown to users are generic — stack traces and internal details stay server-side
- [ ] HTTPS/TLS is enforced; no mixed-content warnings
- [ ] CORS configuration is restrictive (specific origins, not wildcard)

### Dependency & Supply Chain
- [ ] No dependencies with known critical/severe CVEs
- [ ] Lock files (e.g., `package-lock.json`, `requirements.txt`) are up to date
- [ ] Internal packages are pinned to specific versions, not ranged

---

## Performance Review Checklist

### Database & Query Performance
- [ ] N+1 queries are avoided — batch loading or eager loading where appropriate
- [ ] Indexes exist for columns used in `WHERE`, `JOIN`, `ORDER BY`, and `GROUP BY`
- [ ] No expensive operations inside loops (e.g., DB calls, HTTP requests in a `for` loop)
- [ ] Pagination is implemented for list endpoints (cursor-based or offset/limit)
- [ ] Query result sets are limited to what's needed — no `SELECT *` from large tables

### Computation & Memory
- [ ] Large data processing uses streaming/chunking instead of loading everything into memory
- [ ] Recursion depth is bounded or converted to iteration for unbounded inputs
- [ ] Caching is used for expensive, repeatable operations (e.g., Redis, memoization)
- [ ] No unnecessary object allocations in hot paths (e.g., in tight loops)
- [ ] Asynchronous/background processing is used for long-running or non-blocking tasks

### Network & I/O
- [ ] HTTP connections are reused (connection pooling)
- [ ] Response payloads are compressed (gzip/brotli) and sized appropriately
- [ ] Timeouts are set on all external network calls (connect, read, write)
- [ ] Retry logic includes exponential backoff and jitter
- [ ] Static assets are cached, versioned, and served via CDN where appropriate

### Concurrency
- [ ] Shared mutable state is protected by locks or atomic operations
- [ ] Deadlock and race condition scenarios have been considered
- [ ] Thread/goroutine pools have reasonable limits (no unbounded spawning)
- [ ] Context/timeouts are propagated through async chains

---

## Code Style Review Checklist

### Readability & Maintainability
- [ ] Code follows the project's established style guide (PEP 8, ESLint, Prettier, etc.)
- [ ] Functions and methods are small and single-purpose (Single Responsibility Principle)
- [ ] Variable, function, and class names are descriptive and self-documenting
- [ ] Complex logic includes inline comments explaining the *why*, not the *what*
- [ ] No dead code, commented-out blocks, or `TODO`s without tracking tickets

### Structure & Design
- [ ] Imports are organized (stdlib, third-party, local) and unused imports removed
- [ ] Consistent formatting (indentation, line length, spacing) enforced by linters
- [ ] Error handling is explicit and appropriate — no bare `except:` / catch-all blocks
- [ ] Magic numbers and strings are replaced with named constants
- [ ] Long parameter lists are refactored into configuration objects or dataclasses

### Testing & Documentation
- [ ] New code includes unit/integration tests covering happy path, edge cases, and error paths
- [ ] Tests are deterministic — no reliance on timing, random data without seed, or shared mutable state
- [ ] Public APIs and modules have docstrings/README documentation
- [ ] Changes include or update relevant type annotations / type hints
- [ ] Test coverage is maintained or improved (no significant drops)

---

## PR Review Workflow

### Before Starting
1. **Understand the context** — read the PR description, linked issue/ticket, and acceptance criteria
2. **Check the scope** — confirm the changes match the described intent; flag scope creep
3. **Pull the branch** — review the code locally or in CI preview environments when possible

### Review Phases

#### Phase 1: Holistic Scan (5 minutes)
- Skim all changed files to understand the overall structure and risk areas
- Identify files with the highest impact (core logic, auth, data layers) for deeper review
- Note any obvious red flags (large diffs, binary files, auto-generated code being manually edited)

#### Phase 2: Deep Review (time varies)
- Walk through each file systematically
- Run checklists above (Security → Performance → Code Style)
- Verify tests match the changes — every new feature should have corresponding tests
- Check that error handling exists for failure modes introduced by the change

#### Phase 3: Final Verification
- Ensure CI/CD pipelines pass (lint, tests, build, security scans)
- Verify documentation (README, API docs, changelog) is updated if needed
- Confirm migration scripts or schema changes are reversible / have a rollback plan
- Check that environment variables, feature flags, or config changes are documented

### Providing Feedback

| Do ✅ | Don't ❌ |
|------|---------|
| Be specific — reference exact lines and suggest concrete improvements | Leave vague comments like "fix this" or "this is wrong" |
| Explain the *why* behind your suggestion | Dismiss a pattern without explaining trade-offs |
| Separate blockers (must-fix) from nitpicks (nice-to-have) | Mix critical issues with trivial style preferences in the same comment |
| Ask clarifying questions when unsure | Assume intent without asking |
| Praise good patterns and clever solutions | Only comment on negative findings |
| Offer to pair or discuss complex changes | Leave open-ended blockers without discussion |

### Approval Criteria
- **Approve**: No blockers; all concerns are addressed or acknowledged as follow-ups
- **Request Changes**: One or more blocker issues exist (security, correctness, data loss)
- **Comment**: Suggestions or questions only — no blockers, author has discretion

### Post-Merge
- Clean up any temporary branches
- Update tracking tickets and close the associated issue
- Monitor the deployed changes for any regressions or incidents
- Share notable review findings with the team (positive patterns or lessons learned)

---

*Reviewed with ❤️ by Pikachu*
"}
